content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To      : File                                          Date : Feb. 28, 1977


                                                        M-77-39C

From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
       Ken Finney

Subject: Telephone conversation with Connie Kundert, representative
         of the Senate Small Business Committee.

     In a conversation with Connie Kundert, a representative of
the Senate Small Business Committee, she indicated that the
Committee and another state agency (I believe it was General
Services) were going to be sponsoring a social function in
connection with hearings that are going to be conducted by
the Federal Forms Management Committee, which is going to be
in Sacramento. She also indicated that certain items for
the social function were going to be provided by private
groups. For example, the Wine Institute was going to provide
wine, the California Almond Growers were going to provide
complimentary almonds, and certain cheese manufacturers were
going to provide cheese.

     She wondered, first, whether the social function was legally
permissible. I informed her that there would be no prohibition with respect to conducting the function. I also stated
that it would be advisable to tell any of the private entities
that were going to be providing food and beverages that they
should not have their lobbyists, assuming they have lobbyists,
arrange for providing the items.

     Assuming that no lobbyist is involved, I indicated to her
that the only issues then relate to potential reporting obligations. I indicated that the public officials attending the
function conceivably could receive reportable gifts and that
the private entities providing the food and beverage conceivably
could incur reporting obligations if they employ lobbyists.

     She then indicated that the amounts provided would be relatively
small with the possible exception of the wine. In particular,
she did not believe that, in toto, the value of the items provided by any particular entity would exceed $25. I indicated
to her that under these circumstances I did not think any reporting obligations would ensue for the recipients. This was

File re Kundert
p.2
Feb. 28, 1977


based on the notion that no particular recipient would consume
$25 worth of food or drink at the event.

I told her that with respect to any of the private entities
providing goods they could list the amount provided as a lump
sum payment to influence, but they would not have to disclose
any individual recipients since I did not think any individual
recipient would receive $25 worth of food and drink.

It seemed to me that this situation was distinguishable from
the State Lands Commission situation the staff had previously
discussed, primarily because of the limited nature of the
event. In other words, the event was not a three or four-day
function at which a large amount of food and drink and other
items would be provided. To the contrary, it was a single
function at which a small amount of food and drink was going
to be provided.


KF:jo

sections.question_synthetic: Are gifts of food and beverages provided by private entities (Wine Institute, California Almond Growers, cheese manufacturers) to a state-sponsored social function subject to reporting obligations under California political practices law?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: No reporting obligations would be triggered for recipients since no individual attendee would consume $25 worth of food or drink. Private entities providing goods may list amounts as lump sum payments to influence without disclosing individual recipients, provided no lobbyists arrange the contributions and individual gifts remain under the $25 threshold.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an internal FPPC memorandum documenting an informal telephone advice conversation rather than a formal advice letter with structured Q/A sections. No formal QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections present. Extracted question and conclusion from narrative content. Document provides informal guidance on gift reporting thresholds and lobbying involvement concerns.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Are gifts of food and beverages provided by private entities (Wine Institute, California Almond Growers, cheese manufacturers) to a state-sponsored social function subject to reporting obligations under California political practices law?

CONCLUSION: No reporting obligations would be triggered for recipients since no individual attendee would consume $25 worth of food or drink. Private entities providing goods may list amounts as lump sum payments to influence without disclosing individual recipients, provided no lobbyists arrange the contributions and individual gifts remain under the $25 threshold.

For example, the Wine Institute was going to provide wine, the California Almond Growers were going to provide complimentary almonds, and certain cheese manufacturers were going to provide cheese. She wondered, first, whether the social function was legally permissible. I informed her that there would be no prohibition with respect to conducting the function. I also stated that it would be advisable to tell any of the private entities that were going to be providing food and beverages that they should not have their lobbyists, assuming they have lobbyists, arrange for providing the items. Assuming that no lobbyist is involved, I indicated to her that the only issues then relate to potential reporting obligations. I told her that with respect to any of the private entities providing goods they could list the amount provided as a lump sum payment to influence, but they would not have to disclose any individual recipients since I did not think any individual recipient would receive $25 worth of food and drink. It seemed to me that this situation was distinguishable from the State Lands Commission situation the staff had previously discussed, primarily because of the limited nature of the event. To the contrary, it was a single function at which a small amount of food and drink was going to be provided. KF:jo
embedding.summary: FPPC memorandum documenting a telephone conversation with a Senate Small Business Committee representative regarding the legality and reporting obligations for a state-sponsored social function where private industry groups would provide complimentary food and beverages.
